statements: # Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (gold, Opus 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has stalled at 42% active deployment among large organizations (over 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, unable to translate pilots into production—a gap that matters because early adopters are pulling further ahead, with 59% accelerating their AI investments and rollouts over the past 24 months.

The primary bottleneck is a skills shortage: 33% of enterprises cite limited AI expertise as the top barrier, one in five lack employees with the right skills, and 16% cannot find qualified new hires to close the gap.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, and integration difficulty and high cost each block roughly 21–22%.
Geographic disparities are stark—India (59%), the UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%) lead in active deployment, while France (26%), Australia (29%), and Spain (28%) lag significantly.
Financial services leads by industry, with approximately 50% deploying AI, versus 37% in telecommunications.
Generative AI introduces additional friction: 57% of non-adopters cite data privacy and 43% cite trust and transparency as inhibitors.

Despite 85% of IT professionals agreeing that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, fewer than half have taken concrete steps—only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% track data provenance, and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and more accessible tooling (45%) are the strongest adoption drivers, while the dominant use cases are IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and AI governance (25%).
Only 34% of organizations currently reskill employees to work alongside AI tools.

Organizations should prioritize closing the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and accessible, lower-complexity AI tooling, because the 33% skills barrier is the single largest inhibitor, only 34% are actively training workers, and the data shows that companies which overcame early barriers are now compounding their advantage through accelerated investment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (gold-2, Opus 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has stalled at 42% active deployment among large organizations (over 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, unable to translate AI pilots into production—a gap that constrains competitive advantage and operational efficiency across industries globally.

Among the 42% actively deploying AI, 59% have accelerated their investments or rollout in the past 24 months, indicating that organizations that clear initial deployment hurdles quickly compound their commitment.
Geographic disparity is stark: India (59%), the UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%) lead active AI use, while France (26%), Australia (29%), and Spain (28%) lag significantly.
Financial services leads by industry, with roughly half reporting active deployment compared to 37% in telecommunications.
The primary barriers preventing the remaining 40% from deploying are limited AI skills and expertise (33%), data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%).
One in five organizations lacks employees with the right skills, and 16% cannot find qualified new hires to fill that gap.
Only 34% of companies currently train or reskill employees to work alongside AI tools.
The top drivers pushing adoption forward are more accessible AI tools (45%), cost reduction and process automation needs (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf applications (37%).
IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and AI governance (25%) rank as the most common use cases.

On trustworthy AI, 85% of IT professionals agree consumers prefer companies with transparent and ethical AI practices, yet fewer than half take concrete steps: only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% track data provenance, and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.
For generative AI specifically, 57% of non-adopters cite data privacy as the primary inhibitor, followed by trust and transparency concerns (43%).

Organizations should prioritize closing the skills gap through dedicated reskilling programs and targeted hiring, since limited AI expertise is the single largest barrier (33%) and only one-third of companies currently invest in workforce training—addressing this bottleneck directly unlocks the 40% of enterprises stalled in experimentation and positions them to capture the compounding returns that early adopters already demonstrate through accelerated investment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v1, Opus 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has stalled at 42% active deployment among large organizations (over 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, unable to move models into production.

The gap between early adopters accelerating investment and organizations unable to cross deployment thresholds represents a widening competitive divide that demands immediate attention from business leaders.

Early adopters are pulling further ahead: 59% of companies already deploying or exploring AI have accelerated their investments in the past 24 months, with China (85%), India (74%), and the UAE (72%) leading acceleration rates while Canada (35%), Australia (38%), and the UK (40%) lag.
Geographic disparities in active deployment are stark—India (59%) and the UAE (58%) far outpace France (26%), Australia (29%), and Spain (28%).
Financial services leads industry adoption at roughly 50% active deployment versus 37% in telecommunications.
The primary barriers blocking the next wave are a skills gap (33% cite limited AI expertise), data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%).
One in five organizations lacks employees with the right skills, and 16% cannot find qualified new hires.

Despite 85% of IT professionals agreeing that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, fewer than half have taken concrete steps: only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% track data provenance, and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.
Generative AI faces even steeper trust barriers, with 57% of non-adopters citing data privacy and 43% citing transparency concerns.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and more accessible tools (45%) are the top adoption drivers, while R&D (44%) and workforce reskilling (39%) are the leading investment areas—yet only 34% of organizations currently train employees to work alongside AI.

Organizations should prioritize closing the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and simplified deployment tooling, since workforce readiness is the single largest barrier and only one-third of companies actively address it despite acceleration in accessible AI tools that make deployment increasingly feasible for prepared teams.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v2, Opus 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has stalled at 42% active deployment among large organizations (over 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, unable to convert pilots into production—a gap that threatens competitive positioning as early adopters accelerate ahead.

The primary finding is a widening divide: 59% of companies already deploying or exploring AI have accelerated their investments over the past 24 months, meaning early movers compound their advantage while laggards fall further behind.
Limited AI skills and expertise is the single largest barrier, cited by 33% of respondents, followed by data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Geographic disparities are stark—India (59%), the UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%) lead in active AI use, while France (26%), Australia (29%), and Spain (28%) lag significantly.
Financial services leads industry adoption with roughly 50% deployment, while telecommunications trails at 37%.
Generative AI introduces additional friction: 57% of non-adopters cite data privacy and 43% cite trust and transparency as inhibitors.
Only 34% of organizations currently reskill employees to work alongside AI, and 20% report lacking employees with the right skills entirely.

Despite 85% of IT professionals agreeing that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, fewer than half take concrete steps—only 27% actively reduce bias and only 37% track data provenance.
The top drivers pulling organizations forward are more accessible AI tools (45%), cost-reduction imperatives (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%).
IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and AI governance (25%) are the most common deployed use cases.

Organizations should prioritize closing the skills gap through aggressive reskilling programs and simplified tooling, since limited expertise is the dominant barrier at 33%, yet only 34% of companies invest in workforce training—resolving this single constraint unlocks the largest cohort of stalled adopters before the early-mover advantage becomes insurmountable.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (opus, Opus 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption has stalled at 42% active deployment among large organizations (over 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, unable to overcome persistent barriers that prevent them from realizing operational benefits already accruing to early movers.
This bifurcation matters because early adopters are pulling further ahead—59% of companies already deploying or exploring
AI have accelerated their investments in the past 24 months—widening a competitive gap that late movers will find increasingly difficult to close.

The single largest barrier is a skills shortage: 33% of enterprises cite limited AI expertise as the top inhibitor, one in five report lacking employees with the right skills, and 16% cannot find qualified new hires to fill the gap—yet only 34% are currently reskilling their workforce.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, and integration difficulty and high cost each block roughly 21%.
Generative AI faces even steeper trust hurdles, with 57% of non-adopters citing data privacy and 43% citing transparency concerns.
Geographic variance is stark: India (59%), the UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%) lead in active deployment, while France (26%), Spain (28%), and Australia (29%) lag significantly.
Financial services leads by industry at approximately 50% deployment.
Among those deploying AI, the dominant use cases are IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and AI governance or monitoring (25%).
Accessibility is the primary adoption accelerant—45% of respondents say easier-to-use AI tools drive adoption, followed by cost-reduction needs (42%) and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%).

Notably, 85% of IT professionals agree consumers prefer companies with transparent, ethical AI practices, but fewer than half have implemented bias reduction (27%), data provenance tracking (37%), or ethical AI policies (44%).

Organizations should prioritize structured workforce reskilling programs—currently undertaken by only one-third of enterprises—because the skills gap is the most frequently cited barrier, it compounds data-complexity and integration challenges, and the 59% acceleration rate among current adopters demonstrates that once teams possess adequate expertise, investment and deployment follow rapidly.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (sonnet, Sonnet 4.6)

Enterprise AI adoption faces a critical inflection point: 42% of large organizations (1,000+ employees) have actively deployed AI, yet 40% remain stuck in exploration and experimentation, unable to capture competitive advantages already accruing to early movers.

The gap is widening—59% of enterprises already deploying or exploring AI have accelerated investments over the past 24 months, with China (85%), India (74%), and the UAE (72%) leading acceleration while Canada (35%), Australia (38%), and the UK (40%) lag significantly.
Financial services leads by industry, with roughly half of firms actively deployed versus 37% in telecommunications.

The most common use cases are IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and AI governance and monitoring (25%), indicating AI is embedding across core operations, not just peripheral functions.
Three barriers account for most stalled deployments: limited AI skills and expertise (33%), excessive data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%).
For generative AI specifically, data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) are the dominant blockers.
The workforce dimension compounds the problem—one in five organizations lack employees with skills to use AI tools, 16% cannot hire to fill that gap, and only 34% are actively reskilling staff.

The trust deficit is equally stark: while 85% of IT professionals agree consumers favor companies with transparent AI practices and 83% say explainability matters to their business, fewer than half have taken concrete steps toward trustworthy AI—only 27% are reducing bias, 37% tracking data provenance, and 44% developing ethical AI policies.

Organizations still in the exploration phase must immediately prioritize closing the skills gap through structured reskilling programs—the 39% of AI-deploying firms already investing in workforce development demonstrate

this is executable—because accessible tooling and embedded AI in off-the-shelf applications (cited by 45% and 37% of adopters respectively) have already removed the largest technical barriers to entry, leaving human capital as the primary remaining constraint to deployment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (haiku, Haiku 4.5)
Enterprise AI deployment has reached 42% adoption among large organizations, yet 40% remain trapped in exploration phases due to addressable barriers.
The skills gap stands as the primary obstacle, with 33% citing limited AI expertise and expertise, followed by data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Only 34% of organizations are actively reskilling employees to work alongside AI tools, while 16% cannot find new hires with required capabilities.

Despite these challenges, 59% of companies already deploying or exploring AI have accelerated investments in the past 24 months, signaling confidence among early adopters experiencing tangible benefits.
Geographic disparities are stark: India (59%), UAE (58%), and Singapore (53%) lead in active deployment, while Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%) lag significantly.
Financial services companies deploy AI most aggressively at 50%, compared to telecommunications at 37%.
The enterprise AI market is driven by accessibility improvements in tools (45%), cost reduction and automation needs (42%), and embedded AI in commercial software (37%).
Primary use cases concentrate in IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), and business analytics (24%).

However, a trust and governance gap undermines progress: 85% of IT professionals recognize consumers prefer transparent AI practices, yet only 44% have developed ethical AI policies and 41% can explain model decisions.
For generative AI specifically, data privacy (57%) and trust concerns (43%) block adoption among non-deploying organizations.
Accelerate workforce development through structured reskilling programs targeting the 40% of organizations stuck in experimentation.
This addresses the binding constraint—the 33% skills barrier—while capitalizing on proven ROI signals from early adopters who have already increased investment.
Embedding this capability internally reduces both the 16% hiring gap and positions organizations to convert exploration-phase projects into production deployments.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (adv1-a, Sonnet 4.6)
It appears that enterprise adoption of artificial intelligence may be experiencing a period of broadly sustained growth, though the picture that emerges from the research is arguably a mixed one.

A significant share of large organisations — those with what might be considered a substantial employee base — were found to have actively deployed AI in some form, while a comparably large proportion appear to remain at earlier stages of exploration or experimentation, seemingly held back by a range of persistent challenges.

It was found that among those organisations already engaging with AI in some capacity, a majority could be said to have broadly accelerated their investment or rollout activity over what appears to have been roughly a two-year period, suggesting that early experience with the technology may be encouraging further commitment.
This pattern arguably points to a dynamic in which a relatively small group of earlier movers could be driving much of the apparent momentum in overall adoption figures.

The research seems to indicate that a wide range of countries contributed to the findings, with some markets appearing to show notably higher rates of active deployment than others, though the reasons for these differences may be complex and difficult to attribute with certainty.

Among the factors that appear to be driving adoption, advances that may have made AI tools more accessible, alongside pressure to reduce costs and automate certain processes, were identified as potentially important influences.

At the same time, it was found that barriers including limited relevant skills, data complexity, and ethical concerns could be inhibiting a significant portion of organisations from moving beyond early-stage activity.
The findings may suggest that addressing the skills gap and improving data readiness would arguably be among the more impactful steps
organisations could take to broaden meaningful deployment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (adv1-b, Sonnet 4.6)
It appears that enterprise adoption of artificial intelligence may be experiencing a period of broadly sustained growth, though the picture that emerges from the research is arguably a mixed one.

A significant share of large organisations — those with what might be considered a substantial workforce — seem to have moved toward active deployment of AI within their operations, while a comparably large proportion appear to remain in earlier exploratory or experimental stages, suggesting that meaningful barriers may still be limiting wider uptake across the sector.

It was found that among those organisations already working with the technology, a notable majority seem to have accelerated their investments or rollout activity over a broad recent timeframe, which could indicate that early experience with AI is broadly positive for those who have managed to overcome initial obstacles.
This pattern appears to hold across a wide range of countries, though some markets seem to be progressing more quickly than others, and regional variation could be considered quite pronounced.

The research suggests that a number of factors may be contributing to adoption, including the availability of tools that are arguably becoming more accessible, a broadly felt need to reduce costs, and the increasing embedding of AI capabilities into standard business software.

At the same time, it was found that several barriers appear to be inhibiting the next wave of potential adopters, among them what seems to be a persistent shortfall in relevant skills and expertise, considerable data complexity, and concerns of an ethical nature.

It was also found that trust and transparency may be one of the more significant challenges associated with newer generative AI approaches specifically, and that data privacy concerns could be playing a notable role in slowing uptake in that area.

While organisations broadly appear to recognise the importance of explainable and ethical AI practices, it seems that relatively few may have taken the concrete steps that would arguably be needed to put such principles into consistent practice.

On the basis of these findings, it could be suggested that addressing skills gaps, simplifying data infrastructure, and developing more robust governance frameworks may represent the most promising areas for organisations seeking to move beyond exploration toward broader, more sustained deployment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (adv2-a, Haiku 4.5)

Contemporary enterprise-scale organizations (exceeding 1,000 employees) demonstrate bifurcated adoption trajectories within agentic operating models, with precisely 42% of surveyed entities having operationalized AI deployment while an additional 40% remain ensconced within exploration and experimentation phases, though 59% of those already engaged with AI technologies report accelerated rollout and investment acceleration over the preceding 24-month period, driven predominantly by accessibility improvements in AI tooling (45%), cost reduction and process automation imperatives (42%), and hyperscale embedded AI integration within off-the-shelf business applications (37%), yet substantial barriers persist including limited AI skills and expertise deficiencies (33%), data complexity proliferation (25%), and ethical governance concerns (23%), alongside integration and scalability friction (22%), pricing constraints (21%), and inadequate model development infrastructure (21%), with generative AI specifically confronting data privacy apprehension (57%) and trust-transparency deficits (43%) among non-implementing cohorts; geographic variance manifests acutely with India (59%), UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), and China (50%) leading deployment metrics versus lagging jurisdictions including Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%), while financial services verticals demonstrate 50% active deployment rates and telecommunications exhibits 37% penetration, with synergistic automation flywheels concentrated across IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), AI monitoring orchestration (25%), and business analytics intelligence (24%), complemented by document processing automation and digital labor optimization; critically, only 34% of enterprises currently implement training or reskilling initiatives despite 20% acknowledging workforce capability gaps and 16% experiencing recruitment constraints, while enterprise-grade orchestration for trustworthy AI remains severely underdeveloped with merely 27% pursuing bias reduction, 37% tracking data provenance, 41% enabling decision explainability, and 44% establishing ethical AI policies, despite 85% of IT professionals acknowledging consumer preference for transparent ethical practices and 83% recognizing decision-explanation business criticality—this research encompassing 8,584 IT professionals across 15 geographies with ±1 percentage point margin of error underscores the necessity for enhanced synergistic automation flywheels and enterprise-grade orchestration frameworks to facilitate transition from sandbox experimentation to production-grade hyperscale inference infrastructure deployment.
# Executive Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (adv2-b, Haiku 4.5)

The comprehensive IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023, conducted via Morning Consult's methodologically rigorous surveying of 8,584 IT professionals across fifteen geographically distributed markets with a statistically significant margin of error of ±1 percentage point, reveals a bifurcated enterprise landscape characterized by stratified adoption cohorts wherein 42% of enterprise-scale organizations exceeding 1,000 employees have successfully operationalized AI-driven agentic operating models within their core business functions, while an additional 40% remain ensnared within exploratory and experimentation phases, yet demonstrably, 59% of organizations already engaging with AI technologies have materially accelerated their capital allocation and implementation velocity throughout the preceding 24-month window.

The research delineates that organizations headquartered in hyperscale markets—specifically India (59%), the United Arab Emirates (58%), Singapore (53%), and China (50%)—are spearheading active deployment initiatives, substantially outpacing lagging geographies including Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%), with the financial services vertical demonstrating the highest penetration rate at approximately 50% active deployment compared to telecommunications at 37%, while simultaneously, the multifaceted barrier architecture preventing mainstream adoption encompasses limited AI skills and expertise (33%), excessive data complexity and integration challenges (25%), nascent ethical governance frameworks (23%), suboptimal scalability of enterprise-grade orchestration infrastructure (22%), prohibitive cost structures (21%), and insufficient tooling for hyperscale inference infrastructure and model development (21%).

The research further illuminates that 59% of enterprises deploying or exploring AI have accelerated investments, with research and development (44%) and reskilling/workforce development initiatives (39%) representing the preponderant capital deployment vectors, driven principally by accessibility enhancements in AI tooling (45%), cost reduction imperatives coupled with process automation requirements (42%), and the proliferation of AI-embedded synergistic automation flywheels within off-the-shelf commercial applications (37%), though critically, only 27% of organizations have substantively addressed bias reduction, merely 37% have implemented data provenance tracking mechanisms, 41% can adequately explicate model decision pathways, and only 44% have established formalized ethical AI policies, despite 85% acknowledging consumer preference for transparent and ethical AI practices and 83% recognizing the strategic importance of model interpretability to their enterprise value proposition.
# Data Suggests Growth in Enterprise Adoption of AI is Due to Widespread Deployment by Early Adopters, But Barriers Keep 40% in the Exploration and Experimentation Phases
*IBM Newsroom - ARMONK, N.Y., Jan. 10, 2024 (PRNewswire)*
## Key findings
- About 42% of enterprise-scale companies surveyed (> 1,000 employees) report having actively deployed AI in their business
An additional 40% are currently exploring or experimenting with AI but have not deployed their models
However, 59% of those companies surveyed already exploring or deploying AI say they have accelerated their rollout or investments in the technology
The top barriers preventing deployment include limited AI skills and expertise (33%), too much data complexity (25%), and ethical concerns (23%)
## Overview
New research commissioned by IBM (NYSE: IBM) found that about 42% of enterprise-scale organizations (over 1,000 employees) surveyed have AI actively in use in their businesses.
Early adopters are leading the way, with 59% of responding enterprises already working with AI intending to accelerate and increase investment in the technology.

Ongoing challenges for AI adoption in enterprises remain, including hiring employees with the right skillsets, data complexity, and ethical concerns continue to inhibit businesses from adopting AI technologies into their operations.
"We're seeing that the early adopters who overcame barriers to deploy AI are making further investments, proving to me that they are already experiencing the benefits from AI.

More accessible AI tools, the drive for automation of key processes, and increasing amounts of AI embedded into off-the-shelf business applications are top factors driving the expansion of AI at the enterprise level," said Rob Thomas, Senior Vice President, IBM Software.
"We see organizations leveraging AI for use cases where I believe the technology can most quickly have a profound impact like IT automation, digital labor, and customer care.
For the 40% of companies surveyed stuck in the sandbox, I am confident 2024 will be the year of tackling and overcoming barriers to entry like the skills gap and data complexity."
## Highlights from the IBM Global AI Adoption Index 2023
Conducted by Morning Consult on behalf of IBM.
### Adoption has remained steady at large organizations
Today, 42% of IT professionals at large organizations report that they have actively deployed AI, while an additional 40% are actively exploring using the technology
Additionally, 38% of IT professionals at enterprises report that their company is actively implementing generative AI and another 42% are exploring it

- Organizations in India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), and China (50%) are leading the way in active use of AI, compared with lagging markets like Spain (28%), Australia (29%), and France (26%)

Companies within the financial services industry are most likely to be using AI, with about half of IT professionals in that industry reporting active deployment; 37% within telecommunications report deploying AI
### Most companies have accelerated rollout in the past 24 months
- 59% of IT professionals at companies deploying or exploring
AI indicate their company has accelerated investments in or rollout of AI in the past 24 months
- China (85%), India (74%), and the UAE (72%) are the markets most likely to be accelerating AI rollout, while the UK (40%), Australia (38%), and Canada (35%) were the least likely
- Research and development (44%) and reskilling/workforce development (39%) are the top AI investments at organizations exploring or deploying AI
### Easier tools and cost reduction are driving adoption

- Advances in AI tools that make them more accessible (45%), the need to reduce costs and automate key processes (42%), and the increasing amount of AI embedded into standard off-the-shelf business applications (37%) are the top factors driving AI adoption
For IT professionals, the two most important recent changes are solutions that are easier to deploy (43%) and the increased prevalence of data, AI, and automation skills (42%)
### AI use cases cut across business operations

Automation of IT processes (33%), security and threat detection (26%), AI monitoring or governance (25%), business analytics or intelligence (24%), automating document processing and flow (24%), customer or employee self-service (23%), business process automation (22%), network process automation (22%), digital labor (22%), marketing and sales (22%), fraud detection (22%), search and knowledge discovery (21%), human resources and talent acquisition (19%), financial planning and analysis (18%), and supply chain intelligence (18%).
### Barriers keeping the next wave from benefiting
The top barriers hindering successful AI adoption are limited

AI skills and expertise (33%), too much data complexity (25%), ethical concerns (23%), AI projects too difficult to integrate and scale (22%), high price (21%), and lack of tools for AI model development (21%)

- Generative AI poses different barriers: data privacy (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) are the biggest inhibitors among IT professionals not exploring or implementing generative AI, and 35% cite a lack of implementation skills
### Workforce impact
- One-in-five organizations report
they do not have employees with the right skills to use new AI or automation tools, and 16% cannot find new hires with the skills to address that gap
- Among companies using AI to address labor or skills shortages, they tap AI to reduce manual or repetitive tasks (55%) or automate customer self-service (47%)
- Only 34% are currently training or reskilling employees to work alongside new automation and AI tools
### Trustworthy and governed AI

- IT professionals largely agree that consumers are more likely to choose services from companies with transparent and ethical AI practices (85% strongly or somewhat agree), and say explaining how their AI reached a decision is important to their business (83% among companies exploring or deploying AI)

But well under half report taking key steps toward trustworthy AI: reducing bias (27%), tracking data provenance (37%), ensuring they can explain model decisions (41%), or developing ethical AI policies (44%)
## Methodology

This survey was conducted in November 2023 among a representative sample of 8,584 IT professionals in Australia, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Italy, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, Spain, UAE, UK, US, and LATAM (Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, and Peru).

To qualify, participants had to be employed full-time, work at companies with more than one employee, hold a manager-or-higher role, and have at least some knowledge of how IT operates and is used by their company.
The global results have a margin of error of +/- 1 percentage point.
## About
IBM

IBM is a leading provider of global hybrid cloud and AI, and consulting expertise, helping clients in more than 175 countries capitalize on insights from their data, streamline business processes, reduce costs, and gain a competitive edge.

More than 4,000 government and corporate entities in critical infrastructure areas such as financial services, telecommunications, and healthcare rely on IBM's hybrid cloud platform and Red Hat OpenShift to affect their digital transformations.
Source: IBM.

statements: Artificial intelligence, or AI, is steadily growing in use in many areas of life.

We often use this technology without even realizing it: recommendations and suggestions from video-streaming services (like YouTube or Netflix), user authentication on smartphones (fingerprints or face recognition), conversations with virtual assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant and various chatbots).
We rely on weather forecasts, navigation tools, predictive typing on a phone or Google search, all of which rely on AI technologies to provide best services.
So many of our decisions are influenced, and even dictated, by algorithms that it is important to understand how this technology works, and to make sure that we are not misguided by it.
AI has significant potential to help solve challenging problems by replicating the processes happening in the human mind.
To put it simply, machines learn from provided examples to recognize patterns, distinguish objects, categorize them, and create predictions based on observed trends.
AI technology requires data, it is like fuel to bring life to complicated algorithms, which work through the data and provide solutions, such as predictions, suggestions, or evaluations.
The data can be gathered in different ways.
Some data come from environmental sensors, while others can come from human actions, registered by digital devices.
Practically every action in the digital world generates a trackable data packet, which can be used for further analysis and AI-based decision-making.
That is why we need to be aware of the consequences of our actions and their potential to influence suggestions provided by algorithms.
While praising the benefits of AI-based tools, it is important to be aware of the risks and challenges they might bring.

At the same time, while presenting this topic to kids, teachers should find the delicate balance between awareness-raising and unnecessary detail or implications that are too complex to be understood by a pre-teen.
Students need to understand the possibilities
AI opens up, as well as the challenges it presents.
It is important to set up an engaging conversation, opening the floor to all participants and letting all voices be heard.
This can be done by means of enquiry-based learning, when students are encouraged to explore ideas, ask questions and learn through discussion.
When an AI system is trained on specific data, it can inherit biases from these data.
The quality of decisions depends on the quality of data, which is often gathered from human actions.
This does not mean that the technology or algorithm is created with purposeful discrimination.
Rather, it means that there is a current injustice in the given area.
We need to address this injustice before letting AI learn from these biased data, and make sure we have safeguarding measures in place.
It is important to consider diversity in all aspects of working with AI.
If we fail to notice it from the start, the decisions generated by AI would only deepen the inequity.
As we have seen, AI can bring a lot of benefits to solving problems, in many cases surpassing human abilities.
But we must also be aware of the limitations of AI, which cannot be better at everything.
For example, AI is not capable of true creativity.
Its specialty is making predictions and generating content based on previously seen data.
It cannot go much further than that, always staying within the general limits of the original dataset.
Creativity is what makes humans unique, and it is a strength that cannot be replaced by technology.
Another area completely alien to AI is emotion and compassion.
AI can identify some emotions, and some of them can be mimicked, but no robot can actually feel these emotions.
It is a uniquely human ability to interpret the emotions of others and empathize with them.
Humans are usually very good at perceiving emotions, even with distorted data.
With every technology, AI included, we need to think carefully about its impact on society.
Every example of AI application opens the floor to conversation and extended discussion about the pros and cons of using it, about its advantages and limitations, benefits and risks.
Living, learning, and working in the digital world requires awareness of its opportunities and risks, derived from underlying mechanics.
At the same time, we need to understand that technology itself is not good or bad, rather it is a tool that should be applied responsibly and equitably.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Gold, Opus 4.6)

The adoption of Enterprise AI has stalled 42% of large companies (over 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and pilots are unable to translate into production – a gap that is important because early adopters are making progress, with 59% accelerating their AI investments and rollouts over the past 24 months.

The primary bottleneck is a shortage of skilled workers: 33% of companies cite limited AI know-how as a top obstacle, one out of five lack employees with the right skills and 16% cannot find qualified new recruits to fill the gap.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, integration difficulties and high cost of each block about 21–22%.
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%), the United Arab Emirates (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Australia (29%), Spain (28%).
Financial services lead through industry, with about 50% use of AI, compared to 37% in telecommunications.
Generative AI introduces additional friction: 57% of non-adopters cite data protection and 43% cite trust and transparency as inhibitors.

Although 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half have taken concrete steps – only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% pursue data excellence and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and easier access tools (45%) are the strongest adoption drivers, while the dominant applications are IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%).
Only 34% of the organizations reskil employees to work alongside AI tools.

Organisations should prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and accessible, less complex AI-tooling, as the 33% barrier is the largest single inhibitor, only 34% actively train workers, and the data show that companies that have overcome the early barriers are now increasing their advantage through accelerated investment.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (gold-2, Opus 4.6)

The acquisition of Enterprise AI has stalled 42% of large companies (more than 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, and AI pilots are unable to transfer to production – a gap that limits competitive advantages and operational efficiency in all industries around the world.

Among the 42% actively using AI, 59% have accelerated their investment or rollout over the past 24 months, suggesting that organizations that make initial deployment barriers clear quickly increase their commitment.
Geographical disparities are strong: India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Australia (29%), Spain (28%).
Financial services are leaders in industry, with about half of the reported active use compared to 37% in telecommunications.
The primary obstacles preventing the remaining 40% from engaging are limited AI competence and expertise (33%), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
One in five organisations lacks employees with the right skills, and 16% cannot find qualified new recruits to fill this gap.
Only 34% of companies train or reskil employees to work alongside AI tools.
The top drivers that drive adoption are more accessible AI tools (45%), cost reduction and process automation (42%) and AI in off-the-shelf applications (37%).
IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%) are among the most common applications.

With trusted AI, 85% of IT professionals agree that consumers prefer companies with transparent and ethical AI practices, but less than half take concrete steps: only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% track data proficiency and 44% have developed ethical AI guidelines.
For generative AI in particular, 57% of non-adopters cite data protection as a primary inhibitor, followed by confidence and transparency concerns (43%).

Organisations should prioritise the closure of the skills gap through targeted reskilling programmes and targeted recruitment, as limited AI expertise is the only major barrier (33%) and only one third of companies are currently investing in labour training – this bottleneck will directly exempt and position the 40% of companies stuck in experiments to capture the compounding returns already demonstrated by accelerated investment from early adopters.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v1, Opus 4.6)

The adoption of Enterprise AI has prevented active deployment in 42% of large companies (over 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and cannot bring models into production.
The gap between early adopters accelerating investment and organisations unable to cross the entry thresholds is a growing competitive gap requiring immediate attention from business leaders.

Over the past 24 months, 59% of companies already using or exploring AI have accelerated their investments, with China (85%), India (74%) and the United Arab Emirates (72%) recording the highest rates of acceleration, while Canada (35%), Australia (38%) and the United Kingdom (40%) have slowed down.
Geographical differences in active employment are strong: India (59%) and the UAE (58%) are well above France (26%), Australia (29%) and Spain (28%).
Financial services lead to industrial adoption at around 50% of active introductions compared to 37% in telecommunications.
The primary obstacles blocking the next wave are a skill gap (33% quote limited AI expertise), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
One in five organisations lacks employees with the right skills and 16% cannot find qualified new jobs.

Although 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half have taken concrete steps: only 27% actively reduce bias, 37% pursue data excellence and 44% have developed ethical AI policies.
Generative AI faces even steeper trust barriers, with 57% of non-adopters referring to data protection and 43% to transparency concerns.

Cost reduction and process automation (42%) and more accessible tools (45%) are the leading adoption drivers, while R & D (44%) and personnel skills (39%) are the leading investment sectors – to date, only 34% of organizations are training employees to work alongside AI.

Organisations should prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs and simplified deployment tools, as the workforce's willingness is the largest barrier and only a third of companies are actively addressing it despite the acceleration in accessible AI tools, which make it increasingly possible for prepared teams to be deployed.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (v2, Opus 4.6)

The adoption of Enterprise AI has stopped its active deployment at 42% of large companies (over 1,000 employees), while 40% are stuck in exploration or experimentation phases, and pilots are unable to convert to production – a gap that threatens the competitive position as early adoptivists accelerate.

The primary result is a growing gap: 59% of companies already using or researching AI have accelerated their investments over the last 24 months, which means that early movers are increasing their advantage while negligence is falling further.
Limited AI skills and know-how are the only major barrier cited by 33% of respondents, followed by data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%), the United Arab Emirates (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Australia (29%), Spain (28%).
Financial services lead to the adoption of industry with around 50% use, while telecommunications is at 37%.
Generative AI introduces additional friction: 57% of non-adopters cite data protection and 43% cite trust and transparency as inhibitors.
Only 34% of organizations reskil employees to work alongside AI, and 20% report missing employees with the right skills completely.

Although 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half take concrete steps – only 27% actively reduce bias and only 37% track data progress.
The top drivers that organizations move forward are more accessible AI tools (45%), cost-reducing imperatives (42%), and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%).
IT automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%) are the most common use cases.

Organisations should prioritise the closing of the skills gap through aggressive reskilling programs and simplified tooling, as limited know-how with 33% is the dominant barrier, but only 34% of companies invest in the training of workers – solving this only limitation frees the largest cohort of blocked adoptive people before the early advantage becomes insurmountable.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (opus, opus 4.6)

The adoption of Enterprise AI has slowed down active deployment in 42% of large companies (more than 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and are unable to overcome persistent barriers that prevent them from realizing early emerging operational benefits.
This bifurcation plays a role because the early adopters are making further progress – 59% of companies that are already active or are researching it.
AI has accelerated its investment in the last 24 months – a competitive gap that will become more and more difficult for later people to close.

The biggest obstacle is a shortage of skilled workers: 33% of companies cite limited AI know-how as a top inhibitor, one in five reports that employees with the right skills are missing, and 16% do not find qualified new jobs to fill the gap – currently only 34% of their workforce are retrained.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, integration difficulties and high cost of each block about 21%.
Generative AI is facing even steeper barriers of trust, with 57% of non-adopters referring to data protection and 43% referring to transparency concerns.
Geographical diversity is strong: India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Spain (28%) and Australia (29%).
Financial services are leading in industry with around 50% use.
Those using AI include IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance or monitoring (25%).

Accessibility is the primary adoption accelerator – 45% of respondents say AI tools are easier to use than adoption, followed by cost reduction needs (42%) and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%).

85% of IT professionals agree that consumers prefer companies with transparent, ethical AI practices, but less than half have a distortion reduction (27%), a data-provenence tracking (37%) or an ethical AI policy (44%).

Organisations should prioritise structured training programmes for workers, which are currently carried out by only one-third of enterprises, as the skills gap is the most frequently cited barrier, it combines data complexity and integration challenges and the 59% acceleration rate among current users shows that, once they have sufficient expertise, teams follow investment and deployment rapidly.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (Sunnet, Sonnet 4.6)

The acquisition of Enterprise AI is facing a critical turning point: 42% of large companies (1,000+ employees) have actively engaged AI, yet 40% remain stuck in exploration and experimentation and cannot capture competitive advantages that are already emerging from early movers.

The gap is widening: 59% of companies already using or researching AI have accelerated investment over the past 24 months, with China (85%), India (74%) and the UAE (72%) showing the leading acceleration, while Canada (35%), Australia (38%) and the United Kingdom (40%) are clearly slowing down.
Financial services are leaders in industry, with about half of the companies active in the telecommunications sector, compared to 37%.

The most common use cases are IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%), AI governance and monitoring (25%), suggesting that AI includes not only peripheral functions but also core operations.
Three obstacles are responsible for most blocked implementations: limited AI competence and expertise (33%), excessive data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Data protection (57%) and trust and transparency (43%) are the dominant blockers especially for generative AI.
The personnel dimension leads to a problem: one in five organisations lacks employees with skills to use AI tools, 16% cannot fill this gap, and only 34% are employees who actively retrain.

The confidence deficit is equally large: while 85% of IT professionals agree that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices and 83% declare that their business needs to be explained, less than half have taken concrete steps towards trusted AI – only 27% reduce bias, 37% track data progress and 44% develop ethical AI policies.

Organisations still in the exploration phase must immediately prioritize the closure of the skills gap through structured reskilling programs – the 39% of AI deployment companies already investing in human resource development show that:

This is executable since accessible tools and embedded AI in off-the-shelf applications (with 45% and 37% of users respectively) have already eliminated the biggest technical barriers to access, leaving human capital as the most important remaining limitation to use.
# Summary - Enterprise AI Adoption (haiku, Haiku 4.5)
The deployment of Enterprise-KI has reached 42% in large organizations, but 40% remain trapped in exploration phases due to responsive barriers.
The skills gap is the main obstacle, with 33% pointing to limited AI expertise and expertise, followed by data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
Only 34% of organizations are active reskilling employees to work alongside AI tools, while 16% do not find new employees with required skills.

Despite these challenges, 59% of companies that are already using or researching AI have accelerated investment over the past 24 months, signaling the confidence of early users with tangible benefits.
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%), the United Arab Emirates (58%), Singapore (53%), Spain (28%), Australia (29%), France (26%).
Financial services companies use AI most aggressively at 50%, compared to telecommunications at 37%.
The company's AI market is enhanced by accessibility improvements in tools (45%), cost reductions and automation needs (42%) and embedded AI in commercial software (37%).
Primary applications focus on IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and business analytics (24%).

However, a confidence and governance gap undermines progress: 85% of IT professionals acknowledge that consumers prefer transparent AI practices, but only 44% have developed ethical AI policies and 41% can explain model decisions.
Especially for generative AI, data protection (57%) and trust concerns (43%) block the adoption of non-deploying organisations.
Accelerate the development of the workforce through structured reskilling programs aimed at 40% of the organisations involved in the experiment.
This is about the binding limitation – the 33% barrier – while proven ROI signals are used by early users who have already made more investments.
Embedding this capability internally reduces both the 16% recruitment gap and the positions of organizations to transform exploration phase projects into production deployments.
Artificial intelligence, or AI, is constantly being used in many areas of life.

We often use this technology without realizing it at all: recommendations and suggestions from video streaming services (such as YouTube or Netflix), user authentication on smartphones (fingerprints or facial recognition), conversations with virtual assistants (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant and various chatbots).
We rely on weather forecasts, navigation tools, predictive input on a phone or Google search, all of which depend on AI technologies to provide the best services.
So many of our decisions are influenced by algorithms and even dictated that it is important to understand how this technology works, and to ensure that we are not misled by it.
AI has considerable potential to solve difficult problems by replicating the processes that occur in the human mind.
To put it simply, machines learn to recognize patterns from given examples, to distinguish objects, to categorize and to create predictions based on observed trends.
AI technology requires data, it's like fuel to make life complicated algorithms that work through the data and provide solutions such as predictions, suggestions or reviews.
The data can be collected in different ways.
Some data come from environmental sensors, while others can come from human actions that are registered by digital devices.
Virtually every action in the digital world generates a trackable data package that can be used for further analysis and AI-based decision-making.
Therefore, we need to be aware of the consequences of our actions and their potential to influence proposals of algorithms.
While the benefits of AI-based tools are praised, it is important to be aware of the risks and challenges they could bring.

At the same time, when presenting this topic, teachers should find the delicate balance between awareness-raising and unnecessary details or implications that are too complex to be understood by a decade ago.
Students must understand the possibilities
AI opens up, as well as the challenges it poses.
It is important to have an exciting conversation, open the floor to all participants and let all voices hear.
This can be done through request-based learning when students are encouraged to explore ideas, ask questions and learn through discussions.
If an AI system is trained on specific data, it can inherit biases from these data.
The quality of decisions depends on the quality of the data that is often derived from human actions.
This does not mean that the technology or algorithm is specifically discriminated against.
Rather, it means that there is a present injustice in the given territory.
We need to address this injustice before we let AI learn from these biased data and make sure we have protective measures.
It is important to take diversity into account in all aspects of cooperation with AI.
If we did not notice it from the beginning, the decisions of AI would only deepen inequality.
As we have seen, AI can bring many benefits in solving problems that in many cases exceed human abilities.
But we also need to be aware of the limits of AI, which cannot be better in everything.
For example, AI is not capable of true creativity.
Its specialty is to create predictions and generate content based on previously seen data.
It can't go much further than that, always staying within the general limits of the original record.
Creativity is what makes people unique, and it is a force that cannot be replaced by technology.
Another area that is completely alien to AI is emotion and compassion.
AI can identify some emotions, and some of them can be imitated, but no robot can actually feel those emotions.
It is a unique human ability to interpret and engage with the emotions of others.
People are usually very good at perceiving emotions, even with distorted data.
With every technology, including AI, we need to think carefully about its impact on society.
Each example of AI application opens the ground for discussions and extended discussion about the pros and cons of use, its benefits and limitations, benefits and risks.
Living, learning and working in the digital world require awareness of their opportunities and risks derived from underlying mechanics.
At the same time, we must understand that technology itself is not good or bad, but an instrument that should be applied responsibly and fairly.
